speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, you were telling me about your brother who went to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I brought him in hospital there in the ghetto, and I left him. Well I need to come back. And I came back to the concentration camp, back where I was. And I need to go see my sister in the other camp. She was [NON-ENGLISH] camp. Was easier. That was an easier camp there. Was not so strict with us, where I was. There was more children too. As I told her-- no, I went Wednesday. That was Tuesday night they was killed. Wednesday I [? no ?] went. I came back and I was exhausted. And the girls want me to eat something, and I can not eat nothing. Thursday morning I got up and I told the boys and girls to cover me. And they covered. They said, don't worry. Everything be OK here. And I went to see my sister. And I told her, my brother's in the ghetto. When they go-- they used to go with the truck once a week to the ghetto and the concentration camp-- to both. It's to bring clothes. The Germans used to bring pictures. I used to take home stuff. And I said very loudly, is I told her what happened. I no ate from Tuesday 12 o' clock I don't touch nothing Wednesday. Thursday night I came back to my sister, and walking in the kitchen. And I fainted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother came to help. Picked me up. And he went and made me a shot of whiskey to bring me back alive. He say. Don't cry. Nothing help. I'm around, and I watch you. And I say, nobody can [INAUDIBLE]. My mother and my father. And I was very bad. Like a week nobody can talk to me. Nobody can say nothing to me. And everybody was talking. This mother's killed. This father's killed. I said, I don't care. My father and mother is dead. And I have a wounded brother in the ghetto. And I have another sister in the concentration camp. And I'm here. We all three apart now. And my mother, my everybody took us. Sadly a week later, the Gestapo decided to pull in all us in the main concentration camp. No more private. What happened, nobody have a money to pay him to be in private concentration camp. See, we paid like 10,000 marks to be. I know my father paid 100-- 1,500 marks to be private. Not in main, not to be-- the Gestapo was sitting at the balcony. When you passed by the yard, he was shooting you. That the only thing was a little bit released. See, he used to sit of the balcony, and he didn't like you, how you walk, he shot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this was in the main camp you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main camp. In the main camp, we went, was like four weeks in '43 before Passover. After-- after Palm. And [NON-ENGLISH] came my cousin have a family in the ghetto. The Gestapo used to pull together like 1,000 marks a Sunday morning, and get passes to go and get to visit the parents, family. It was one Sunday morning, my boyfriend say, we're going-- I go visit my mother in ghetto. and my sister. You're coming along with me. And he paid 100 mark for me. And I went to see my brother. When my cousin came back from the city where the ghetto was. And he say to me, [NON-ENGLISH], your brother is very sick of typhus. He was burning up. That was Thursday. It's Sunday. My boyfriend figured I need to go see my brother. He can not take more. I can't take that more. And I went, came in to my cousin's in the apartment. And I saw my brother. The lice where the bandage was-- the hospital sent him-- after five days they sent him to my cousins, from the hospital to my cousin in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lice was so big walking around off his arm. The bandage was small. And I start to holler at my cousin. I say, why you not took him in hospital, change the bandages? It's four weeks the same bandages. It's infection and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took my brother of the back, my back. And took him in hospital. Was like a block from my cousin's where they was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took him in hospital. I came in in hospital. The nurse said, what you want here? I said, I want a bucket of water with alcohol. I want your change in clothes. You have here plenty of clothes. I don't have a clothes there to change him. I say, I want clothes. I want a bucket of water with alcohol. I want you. And I want The doctor. How come the doctor not told my brother to come changing the bandages? And I want to see the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SHOUTING]

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, who you are to tell me what to do? I say, I am from the concentration camp. I Gestapo. I said, I've been-- went to hell. And I want you right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she say, I haven't no time for you. I went to her, catched her by the shirt. And I shaked her. I was strong. I was not a weak little girl. And I shaked her. I say you better do it right now. We cleaned up my brother. She came. She was afraid from me. She came with the bucket of water, with alcohol. We washed him around. We put clean clothes. We put clean bandage. I washed around. The fever went down right away from the typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, what can make a [INAUDIBLE] boy would have typhus? I can catch it and bring it in concentration camp. That time the girls kill me. Huh? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But thank God God was good to me. I was immune to that. Never catched it. See, my husband-- that time he was not my husband-- he was with typhus sick. A lot of the guys were sick with typhus. And the only one what I can take-- I was immune. Every girl was afraid to catch it. And-- in June, Shavuot time, they went and liquidate the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: They liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. And my brother was hiding in a bunker for four days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he came, not to my concentration camp, where he knows mine is very bad. He went in the concentration camp where my sister was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought for sure I took him [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was again like a maniac. I was throwing things. I was working in the-- of the machine. The Gestapo comes up to me. He say, [NON-ENGLISH] what are you doing now? I said, nothing. Leave me alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: But to that Gestapo you can talk. You know, he was just like our-- he took care of you. You give him 100 marks, he took care of you very good. He loved you for that. See, sometime, you pushed him money. That's all. My boyfriend was off a bike. Well he went to the city to tell the guys to come with the wagons for bring the stones to the machine. I see he's coming down the hill, killing himself with the bike. Was a hill there, like [NON-ENGLISH] and screaming, [? oh-ho, ?] your brother is alive. He is in [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said what are you talking? You're crazy. He said, oh no. I'm not crazy. Today is Saturday. Tomorrow we go see your brother. That was like Monday, Sunday they liquidate the ghetto. And Sunday-- Saturday, he came to the city where he was born. He was raised. He wanted a piece of bread, something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: The neighbor, the good friends-- neighbors-- not give him, not a drink of water. Nobody will let him in the house. OK. He came there. And I went to see him. He was tiny, skinny. He was a little boy, 13 years old. He no got nobody, nothing, and so much suffering. [INAUDIBLE] I went back. I need to be back. They left us out 6:00, the Gestapo. 12:30 I need to be back to walk in with the group back in camp. I got the Gestapo not to see us late. I came back, and I was glad my brother is OK. I saw my brother. I saw my sister. And everybody say, now you look better. I say, oh no. I don't look better, I say. I just feel like something trapped in me. That time I felt worse. Well, when I saw my brother, and I saw my sister, what happened to my father and mother. The worst thing to happen to me when my mother and father was killed. I lost my grandpa. He was beaten. He was dead, died from the Gestapo. That went to Auschwitz, my grandma, and my cousins. It was something [INAUDIBLE]. But that was bad. But after awhile you settled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was not enough. Came the policeman who was working for us. He was in a different concentration camp --off a bike. And he said to the Gestapo, I want to see this girl. I know her. I want to talk to her. And the Gestapo say, why? You want to go to sleep with her in the wheat, in the field.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, maybe. See, I know her from baby when she was growing up. And I like her. And I'd like to talk her a little bit. Give her a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, are you bringing her back? He said, for sure I bring her back. And we went like 100 yards. And he say, Rushka, it's no good. I wish I can take you home to my house, but my wife is jealous.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, I can't do that. The only thing we can do, arrange, tell your friend, and tell-- the friend should tell other friends-- and try to tell your sister and brother, more people to run away from the camp-- escape. Well they're starting to shoot in the camps now, the Gestapo. That was 1943, in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to warn you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not him, I don't think we would make it. We make it like 100 people. And we start to run away from camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? The guy what was working with us, tell us what to do, his name was Werzbech. He was tough. He was Jewish. He was tough. He used to keep with the Germans more. He thought he keep with the Germans, they'll let him live. But anyway, he ran away too. And he want-- he find out a little bit-- some week went by. And he said, oh no.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he kept me in the concentration camp, my boyfriend not run away. And lots of people not go. Well, me, I and my boyfriend, he'd say, we all do bad things. We used to do bad things. We used-- he used to bring in bread for the people, my boyfriend. He used to do like some things. He used to, I mean, we used to acknowledge a little bit. Go out a ways. He want to keep me in the concentration camp. My boyfriend comes up to him. He say, Werzbech, I'll tell you what. Leave her this morning out. You can keep her tomorrow. I'll bring you a loaf of bread and a pint butter. Good tonight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that covers everything for you. All right? He took a wagon with horses. His mother-- my boyfriend's mother-- ran away from the ghetto. And she was hiding not too far from the concentration camp by a guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very good, the guy. He helped a lots of Jewish people. He used to have a big yard with apple trees. He used to get bushel of apples, put it out of sight of the highway, of everybody should get an apple. Just something fruit. And he kept his mother. He picked up his mother, and ride and him went with the horse and wagon in the hiding woods, '43, July. End of July, it was that, I think. We supposed to meet-- was organized. We supposed to meet a group there. Everybody's supposed to be there and there, and meet. But that's not happened like that. Forget that. We came to the hiding woods like about 2 o' clock. About 5:00, God say, wait a minute. You can't stay dry. It starts a-raining. We're outside, raining, pouring. We have nothing to cover ourselves, nothing to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're drenched, wet, hungry. And my boyfriend say, you know what? Stay with my mother here under that tree. Don't move too far. When you move far, I will find you. And I go in the city, in the country there, and get a loaf of bread, something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's supposed to come home and come back in two hours. It's three hours. It's four hours. No Saul. Nobody. No group. No Saul, nobody. He went-- he was afraid to go into the house daytime. He went [INAUDIBLE] in the stable there and fell asleep. He was dry. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brother and your sister with you when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brother was in the other camp. My sister ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hiding woods with me. But my brother, the Gestapo catched him, and put him on the truck. And was a [INAUDIBLE] all up a hill. And that's where they took shot all from the concentration camp what was left. Was about 4,000 people they took concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we were [INAUDIBLE] we heard from other people that ran away later in a couple of days. Come together, sure. We know that when we came back, 1944, we went to the hill to see that. And the guy lived not too far, he say, six weeks after was already covered everything. The hair starts to come up from the grave, you know what covered. And he swore. He say for three, four weeks, the-- the dirt was moving as people were still maybe under the dirt alive. That's what he told us. Sure, my brother is there between 4,000 people of the hill. My uncle, my mother's brother, and my cousins, friends when we was in concentration camp. A lots of people was there what we know, you know. They was killed. Just plain killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they will Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who was doing the shootings? Do you  know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they German or Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans-- all German. They came, more Gestapos was. Usually was two or three Gestapos in concentration camp. But after was like, we saw when something happened, or came more five Gestapos. And we decided that's no good. And after awhile came 10 Gestapos. I said that was worse. I said would they do something? And when they liquidated the ghetto, we know something happened with the concentration camps already. And one guy said to my boyfriend-- at that time he was-- he say, Saul, watch while the Russian beating very bad the Germans. They're pushing back. And it be problem. That's what he said. We expected we're [? getting ?] into a problems. We were still one year in the hiding woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you arrived in the woods. And you said your sister was able to escape and join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She escaped in another place. She was with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. She was not part of your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not part of my group. She was with another group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep in touch with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, we saw each other every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till December. Like in middle December she was shot from the Gestapo in the hiding woods. They like circled, the Gestapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Surrounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surrounded. Not once. They circled about four times. They circled one time. We was on one hill. And I was-- we was the bottom hill. And I was up the top hill. They was afraid to shoot to us. And you wouldn't want to bother them, till went a whole day. Night time they left. They don't bother us. They figured they'd come back another day. But we never stayed overnight. We went in another place hiding woods. And that time my sister was shot. Was winter time. She was dumb. And she moved out from the [? grave-- ?] from the bunker. They called bunker. You know, under the ground. And she-- she thought if she ran away, they would catch them there. And she was shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your life in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the woods we went stealing stuff. We went home to where which was born to pick up, from the neighbors, some food. But the food was-- one neighbor, we came in. I went to my neighbors. And my boyfriend went to his neighbors. And there was Gestapo playing cards. And he knocked on the door. And he-- she say, the Jews came to rob us. And he wanted just a loaf of bread from her. And Gestapo-- and he heard the Gestapo. She hollered to the Gestapo. He ran away. And we supposed to meet by a neighbor under the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like stable. We make arrangement about. And here I stood there and waited. And I heard he was running to the hill. And was a-- a river, a small river, with trees-- big trees. And was a train tracks where a train used to go by. And he went around, and hided between the train. And I wanted to run after. And I thought I'd be caught. I jumped in in the river-- swear to God, with my true-- in the river, and hided under a tree. The shadow hided me. Well I stayed in the river like that. Was December, freezing. The Gestapo came five feet from me. He say [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews run away from us. And I-- I was just afraid I should not cough. And when he [INAUDIBLE] left back, he was between the train, between the train rack. A train came in. He was lying flat. And the train passed by. He was a afraid to get up. Was too-- too fast. The Gestapo not to see him, shoot him. And it was quick. That happened like half an hour, the whole story. And when we came back, we come together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He took off his long underwear, give me to put it on. Well I was soaking wet, freezing. And he put on his slacks. My shoes came apart of the [INAUDIBLE] from wetness. And I came in the hiding wood back. But I brought the fruit. I not drop that, or we no have nothing to eat. That was important. I brought beans. I got flour. And I got a loaf of bread. He no got nothing. And we came back and I catched a very bad cold. And I was coughing. I thought, I'll never make it. I was bad off. With hot water and with hot towels wrapping the neck that time, I pulled through.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And at that time, what type of shelter did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the ground, we digged, called-- called bunkers. That's where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were living in your bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was eight. When one person turned, we turned. All, we need to turn all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big, approximately, was the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was like eight feet long and eight feet wide. We was cooking outside of a branch. [INAUDIBLE] pulled the branch, and we was cooking. We needed to build the fire very hot, not smoke to show. You're looking. I'll tell you, believe me. I can't believe it what I'm telling you that everything now. And I remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
